title: Practical Management in Coronary In-Stent Restenosis: A Narrative Review
one_line_summary: A mechanism-guided, substrate-driven approach to coronary in-stent restenosis management emphasizing intracoronary imaging to identify the dominant mechanism before selecting lesion preparation and definitive therapy with drug-coated balloons or repeat drug-eluting stents.
key_pearls: ISR is mechanistically heterogeneous; treatment must target the dominant mechanism (mechanical vs. biological) identified by intracoronary imaging (IVUS/OCT) rather than applying a uniform strategy.
In BMS-ISR, DCB and repeat DES have similar 3-year efficacy, but in DES-ISR, repeat DES is moderately more effective at reducing TLR, though DCB avoids adding further metal layers.

For patients treated with DCB alone, DAPT can be shortened to **1 month** in stable patients, making it preferable in high bleeding risk, whereas repeat DES requires a minimum **1 month** and typically **6–12 months** of DAPT.

Intravascular imaging-guided PCI for ISR carries a Class I (ESC 2024) or Class IIa (ACC/AHA 2021) recommendation, but this is based on observational data; no ISR-specific RCT has demonstrated a reduction in hard clinical outcomes.
Vascular brachytherapy (VBT) is a Class IIb option for recurrent multilayer DES-ISR, delivering antiproliferative therapy without adding metallic layers, but availability is limited.

Optimal lesion preparation is a prerequisite: high-pressure non-compliant balloons, scoring/cutting balloons, IVL for calcific underexpansion, or atheroablative therapy should precede definitive DCB or DES treatment.
Recurrent ISR requires mechanistic reassessment with imaging; multilayer ISR should prompt consideration of metal-free strategies (DCB, VBT) or CABG via Heart Team discussion.
population: Patients with coronary in-stent restenosis (BMS-ISR and DES-ISR), including complex subsets (CTO-ISR, LM-ISR, SVG-ISR, recurrent ISR)

sections.heading: Definition and Incidence of In-Stent Restenosis
sections.content:
- ARC-2 defines ISR as ≥**50%** luminal diameter stenosis within the stented segment or **5 mm** proximal/distal edges, or >**75%** cross-sectional area reduction on intravascular imaging.
- Clinically relevant ISR requires recurrent ischemic symptoms, objective ischemia (FFR ≤ **0.80**, iFR ≤ **0.89**), or angiographic stenosis >**70%** in asymptomatic patients.
- Contemporary DES platforms have an annual ISR risk of approximately **2%**, rising substantially in higher-risk subsets (small vessels, long lesions, bifurcations).

sections.heading: Classifications of ISR
sections.content:
- **Mehran Classification (angiographic):** Type I (Focal, ≤**10 mm**); Type II (Diffuse intra-stent, >**10 mm**); Type III (Diffuse proliferative, >**10 mm** extending beyond edges); Type IV (Occlusive). Focal lesions respond well to DCB; complex lesions often need adjunctive modification or surgery.
- **Waksman Classification (mechanism-based, imaging-guided):** Type I (Mechanical: underexpansion, fracture); Type II (Biological: NIH, neoatherosclerosis); Type III (Mixed); Type IV (CTO). Guides treatment: mechanical ISR responds to high-pressure BA and DCB; biological ISR requires antiproliferative therapy (DES/DCB).
- **SCAI 2023 Time-Based Classification:** Early (≤**30 days**, technical/procedural); Late (**30 days–1 year**, biological); Very Late (>**1 year**, neoatherosclerosis).

sections.heading: Risk Factors for ISR
sections.content:
- **Patient factors:** Diabetes mellitus (increases ISR risk **~2–3 fold**), older age, hypertension, dyslipidemia, CKD, smoking, prior CABG. Elevated lipoprotein(a) ≥**30–50 mg/dL** associated with **~1.5 fold** increased risk.
- **Procedural factors:** Stent underexpansion (IVUS MSA <**5.5 mm²** non-LM, <**6.0 mm²** LM; OCT MSA <**4.5 mm²**), malapposition, suboptimal lesion preparation, stent recoil.
- **Anatomical factors:** Lesion length >**20 mm**, vessel diameter <**3 mm** (restenosis rate **20%** vs. **15%** in larger vessels), bifurcation lesions, ostial lesions, CTO, severe calcification (arcs >**180°** or thickness >**0.5 mm** associated with **5×** higher ISR risk), SVG grafts.
- **Stent-related factors:** BMS vs. DES (neoatherosclerosis occurs more frequently and earlier in DES), strut thickness (ultrathin <**70 µm** reduces target lesion failure by **16%** at 1 year), stent fracture (incidence **1–8%**, revascularization rates **15–60%**), stent gaps.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology of ISR
sections.content:
- Inflammation post-PCI activates pro-inflammatory cytokines, stimulating VSMC proliferation and migration, initiating restenosis.
- **Neointimal Hyperplasia (NIH):** Smooth muscle cell accumulation and extracellular matrix deposition; peak late lumen loss in BMS at **6–8 months**, gradual accumulation in DES persisting up to **5 years**.
- **Neoatherosclerosis:** Lipid-laden macrophages, necrotic core, calcifications within neointima; common in very late ISR (>**1 year** post-DES) due to delayed endothelial healing from antiproliferative drugs.
- Antiproliferative agents in DES and DCB target VSMC proliferation and inflammation, mitigating early NIH but may predispose to late neoatherosclerosis.

sections.heading: Diagnosing ISR: Intracoronary Imaging
sections.content:
- **Coronary Angiography:** Initial test but limited by 2D nature; physiological assessment (FFR ≤ **0.80**, iFR ≤ **0.89**) determines reintervention need when angiographic severity is uncertain.
- **IVUS:** Visualizes external elastic lamina for optimal expansion; MSA thresholds predictive of ISR: <**5.5 mm²** non-LM, <**6.0 mm²** LM. Optimal MSA goals: **6 mm²** LAD ostium, **7 mm²** LM bifurcation, **8 mm²** LM PCI. Relative stent expansion ≥**80%** (ideal **90%**). Lower axial resolution (~**150 µm**) limits fine tissue characterization.
- **OCT:** Higher resolution (~**15 µm** axial) for **superior** neointimal characterization (NIH, neoatherosclerosis, thrombus, edge dissection). MSA <**4.5 mm²** and MSA/reference lumen area <**70%** associated with higher ISR risk. Limited tissue penetration restricts deep vessel wall assessment.
- **Practical Framework:** IVUS preferred for vessel sizing, deep plaque burden, calcific underexpansion; OCT preferred for fine structural detail, thrombus, edge dissection, neoatherosclerosis. Both are complementary.
- **Guideline Recommendations:** ESC 2024 assigns imaging-guided PCI a Class I, Level B recommendation for ISR; ACC/AHA 2021 assigns Class IIa, Level B. Evidence is largely observational; practical barriers include cost, availability, and operator expertise.

sections.heading: Management of ISR: Lesion Preparation and Definitive Therapy
sections.content:
- **Balloon Angioplasty (BA):** High recurrence as stand-alone therapy; used as lesion preparation, especially high-pressure non-compliant balloons for underexpansion.
- **Cutting/Scoring Balloons:** Improve lesion preparation in fibrotic/resistant ISR; ISAR-DESIRE 4 trial showed scoring balloon pre-dilation before DCB improved angiographic outcomes.
- **Intravascular Lithotripsy (IVL):** Modifies calcified plaque via sonic pressure waves; useful for calcium-mediated underexpansion when conventional balloons fail; evidence limited to small series.
- **Atheroablative Therapy:** Rotational atherectomy (RA) for calcium arcs >**270°** and >**0.67 mm** thickness; ELCA for NIH ablation; orbital atherectomy experience limited. Niche adjuncts, not routine treatments.
- **DCB vs. Repeat DES:**
  - DAEDALUS meta-analysis (10 RCTs, 1976 patients): DCB and DES have similar 3-year efficacy in BMS-ISR; in DES-ISR, DES moderately more effective at reducing TLR.
  - DCB preferred in multilayer ISR, long/diffuse segments, bifurcation lesions, high bleeding risk (shorter DAPT).
  - Repeat DES preferred when mechanical failure requires scaffolding correction, original underexpansion unresolved, or prior DCB failed.
  - Ultrathin-strut DES associated with reduced TLR and TVR compared to thin-strut DES and DCB at 3 years (observational data).
  - SELUTION4ISR trial: sirolimus-coated balloon non-**inferior** to standard of care at 1 year.
- **Vascular Brachytherapy (VBT):** Delivers localized beta-radiation; Class IIb recommendation (ACC/AHA) for recurrent multilayer DES-ISR; associated with lower MACE at 3 years in observational studies.
- **CABG:** Class IIa recommendation for LM-ISR, multivessel disease, recurrent ISR; arterial grafts show higher patency; Heart Team decision-making **essential**.

sections.heading: Pharmacological Therapies and Risk Factor Modification
sections.content:
- **DAPT Duration:**
  - Repeat DES: minimum **1 month**, typically **6–12 months** depending on presentation (stable vs. ACS) and bleeding risk.
  - DCB alone: **1 month** DAPT in stable patients, preferable in high bleeding risk.
  - ACS-related ISR: longer DAPT regardless of device.
  - PRODIGY trial subgroup: prolonged DAPT reduced MACE at **24 months** (7.3% vs. 16.7%) in ISR patients, but this is a post hoc analysis.
  - Use PRECISE-DAPT and ARC-HBR scores to identify high bleeding risk.
- **Lipid-Lowering Therapy:** High-intensity statin **recommended**; PCSK9 inhibition may be warranted if LDL targets unmet. No direct RCT evidence that intensive lipid-lowering reduces ISR; PIECES-OCT trial evaluating PCSK9 inhibitor effect on strut coverage (ongoing).
- **Risk Factor Optimization:** Blood pressure control, glycemic management, smoking cessation; poor glycemic control and elevated lipoprotein(a) associated with increased recurrence.

sections.heading: Management of ISR in Special Situations
sections.content:
- **CTO-ISR:** Accounts for **10–15%** of CTO PCIs; higher comorbidity burden; technical success ~**85%** comparable to de novo CTO, but higher 12-month MACE. Requires dedicated CTO operators; imaging to clarify mechanism; repeat DES preferred if scaffolding correction needed.
- **LM-ISR:** Repeat LM revascularization independently associated with increased **mortality** (EXCEL trial). Define mechanism with imaging; underexpansion may respond to optimization and repeat DES; CABG preferred for recurrent/complex LM-ISR (Class IIa).
- **SVG-ISR:** High risk of distal embolization and no-reflow; evidence base sparse (DIVA, PROCTOR trials not ISR-specific). Native-vessel revascularization preferred (Class IIa); repeat SVG PCI when native PCI not feasible.
- **Recurrent ISR:** Requires mechanistic reassessment with IVUS/OCT. Multilayer ISR: each additional stent layer reduces expandability and drug penetration; prefer metal-free strategies (DCB, VBT). VBT Class IIb for recurrent multilayer DES-ISR. Optimize modifiable risk factors. Heart Team discussion for CABG if repeated PCI fails.
- **Stent Fracture:** Suspect at tortuosity, hinge points (RCA), long overlapping stents. Confirm with IVUS/OCT. Focal fractures: lesion preparation + DCB or targeted repeat DES. Complete/disrupted fractures: repeat DES to restore scaffolding.

sections.heading: Future Directions
sections.content:
- **Sirolimus-based DCB platforms:** SELUTION4ISR trial (sustained-release sirolimus DCB) showed non-inferiority at 1 year; head-to-head comparisons with paclitaxel-DCB needed.
- **Lesion preparation tools:** Second-generation IVL, refined orbital atherectomy and laser systems under evaluation; currently no ISR-specific randomized evidence.
- **AI-assisted imaging analysis:** Automated quantification of stent expansion, NIH volume, neoatherosclerosis characterization; early proof-of-concept, prospective validation needed.
- **Medical therapy gaps:** Optimal DAPT duration after repeat DES for ISR unstudied in dedicated trials; PCSK9 inhibitors and colchicine (LoDoCo2, COLCOT) not specifically evaluated in ISR populations.
- **Critical evidence gap:** Absence of large ISR-specific RCTs with hard clinical endpoints; most trials use angiographic surrogates (late lumen loss, binary restenosis at **6–12 months**) that do not reliably predict long-term outcomes.

strengths_limitations:
- Strengths: Provides a comprehensive, mechanism-guided, and substrate-driven framework aligned with major international guidelines (ESC 2024, ACC/AHA 2021, JCS/JSCVS 2018); **critically** appraises evidence quality, explicitly distinguishing between RCT data, observational studies, and expert consensus; addresses nuanced clinical decision-making (DCB vs. DES, DAPT duration, special populations).
- Limitations: As a narrative review, it lacks systematic review methodology and quantitative synthesis; recommendations for imaging guidance and specific device choices rely heavily on observational data and surrogate endpoints rather than ISR-specific RCTs with hard clinical outcomes; some discussed therapies (VBT, atheroablative techniques) have limited evidence bases.

drugs_doses.dose: Drug-coated balloon (DCB) platform; dose varies by device
drugs_doses.indication: Treatment of coronary in-stent restenosis (BMS-ISR and DES-ISR), particularly in focal disease, multilayer ISR, or when avoiding additional metal layers is desired
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: No specific adverse effects detailed; general PCI risks apply

drugs_doses.dose: Drug-coated balloon (DCB) platform (e.g., SELUTION SLR with biodegradable polymer matrix for sustained release); dose varies by device
drugs_doses.indication: Treatment of coronary in-stent restenosis; SELUTION4ISR trial demonstrated non-inferiority to standard of care at 1 year
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: No specific adverse effects detailed; general PCI risks apply

drugs_doses.dose: Aspirin plus P2Y12 inhibitor; duration: 1 month (DCB alone, stable patients) to 6–12 months (repeat DES, ACS presentation)
drugs_doses.indication: Post-PCI for ISR to reduce thrombotic risk; duration individualized by device type, clinical presentation, and bleeding risk (PRECISE-DAPT, ARC-HBR scores)
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Bleeding risk; use shorter courses in high bleeding risk patients, elderly, those on oral anticoagulation

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; high-intensity statin therapy per guideline-directed secondary prevention
drugs_doses.indication: Secondary prevention in patients with established CAD and ISR; role of neoatherosclerosis in late ISR supports intensive lipid-lowering
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Standard statin-related adverse effects (myopathy, transaminase elevation)

drugs_doses.dose: Not specified; may be warranted in patients remaining above LDL targets despite maximally tolerated statin
drugs_doses.indication: Potential role in reducing neoatherosclerosis progression in ISR; PIECES-OCT trial evaluating effect on stent strut endothelial coverage (ongoing)
drugs_doses.adverse_effects: Injection site reactions; long-term safety profile favorable in secondary prevention trials

drugs_doses.dose: Low-dose (0.5 mg daily per LoDoCo2 and COLCOT trials)
drugs_doses.indication: Potential anti-inflammatory strategy to reduce NIH and ISR recurrence; not specifically evaluated in ISR populations but demonstrated mortality benefit in stable CAD and post-ACS
